title: 03 — QLoRA Fine-Tuning (Local GPU)
text:
Purpose: Run QLoRA training locally on an Ada / Ampere GPU (≥ 20 GB VRAM).
All training logic lives in src/training/train.py.
This notebook orchestrates the two runs and generates report figures.
Requirements: - NVIDIA Ada or Ampere GPU with ≥ 16 GB VRAM (tested on 20 GB Ada) - flash-attn installed (STEP 2) — provides ~3–4× speedup over eager attention - HF_TOKEN and WANDB_API_KEY in a .env file at the repo root (STEP 3)

import logging
import torch
import yaml
import pandas as pd

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(levelname)s %(message)s')

with open(REPO_ROOT / 'params.yaml') as f:
    params = yaml.safe_load(f)

# GPU info
if torch.cuda.is_available():
    for i in range(torch.cuda.device_count()):
        props = torch.cuda.get_device_properties(i)
        cc = f'{props.major}.{props.minor}'
        fa2 = 'flash-attn2 OK' if props.major >= 8 else 'no flash-attn2 (need cc>=8.0)'
        print(f'GPU {i}: {props.name}  {props.total_memory/1e9:.1f} GB  cc={cc}  [{fa2}]')
else:
    print('WARNING: No CUDA GPU found')

# Parquet checks
for split in ('train', 'val', 'test'):
    p = PROCESSED_DIR / f'{split}.parquet'
    if p.exists():
        df = pd.read_parquet(p)
        print(f'{split}.parquet: {len(df)} studies — OK')
    else:
        print(f'MISSING: {p} — run src.data.load / labels / split first')

# Image spot-check (default local path)
images_dir = REPO_ROOT / params['data']['images_dir'] / 'images_normalized'
train_df = pd.read_parquet(PROCESSED_DIR / 'train.parquet')
has_frontal = train_df['frontal'].apply(lambda x: hasattr(x, '__len__') and len(x) > 0)
print(f'Studies with frontal image: {has_frontal.sum()}/{len(train_df)}')
if has_frontal.sum() > 0:
    sample = list(train_df[has_frontal].iloc[0]['frontal'])[0]
    img_path = images_dir / sample
    print(f'Image spot-check: {img_path}  (exists={img_path.exists()})')
    if not img_path.exists():
        print('WARNING: images not found — check params.yaml data.images_dir')

title: 03 — QLoRA Fine-Tuning (Local GPU)
section: STEP 5 — Run 1: Uniform sampling (no shift correction) [v2: lr=5e-5, 2 epochs]Flash Attention 2 + VRAM-aware batch scaling are applied automatically by train.py. Expected time on 20 GB Ada: ~2–3 h for 2 epochs.v2 changes vs v1: LR 2e-4 → 5e-5, epochs 3 → 2, warmup_ratio 0.03 → 0.06 Rationale: v1 peaked at epoch 1 and macro F1 fell 57% below zero-shot — classic symptom of aggressive LR on a small dataset (~2400 samples).

from peft import PeftModelfrom src.training.model import load_model_and_processorimport torchfrom PIL import ImageSPOT_VARIANT = 'uniform'   # change to 'weighted' to check the other runN_SAMPLES    = 5_SYSTEM_PROMPT = (    "You are an expert radiologist. "    "Write only the Findings section of a radiology report for the chest X-ray shown. "    "Be concise and clinical. Do not include an Impression section.")_BLANK = Image.new('RGB', (224, 224), color=(128, 128, 128))# Locate checkpoint — try versioned name first (post run_name bugfix), then plain name_candidates = [    CHECKPOINT_DIR / f'qlora_{SPOT_VARIANT}_v2' / 'best_model',    CHECKPOINT_DIR / f'qlora_{SPOT_VARIANT}'     / 'best_model',]adapter_path = next((p for p in _candidates if p.exists()), None)if adapter_path is None:    raise FileNotFoundError(f'No best_model found — checked: {_candidates}')print(f'Adapter: {adapter_path}')# Load val setval_df_spot = pd.read_parquet(PROCESSED_DIR / 'val.parquet')val_df_spot = val_df_spot[    val_df_spot['findings'].notna() & (val_df_spot['findings'].str.strip() != '')].reset_index(drop=True)images_dir = REPO_ROOT / params['data']['images_dir'] / 'images_normalized'# Load base model + fine-tuned adaptersprint('Loading base model + adapter (may take ~1 min)...')spot_model, spot_proc = load_model_and_processor(    model_id=params['model']['base_model_id'],    quantization=params['model']['quantization'],    device_map='auto',)spot_model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(spot_model, str(adapter_path))spot_model.eval()print('Ready.\n')for i, (_, row) in enumerate(val_df_spot.head(N_SAMPLES).iterrows()):    # Load frontal image (blank fallback)    pil_image = _BLANK    frontal = row.get('frontal', [])    if hasattr(frontal, '__len__') and len(frontal) > 0:        img_path = images_dir / list(frontal)[0]        if img_path.exists():            try:                pil_image = Image.open(img_path).convert('RGB')            except Exception:                pass    indication = str(row.get('indication', '') or '').strip()    if indication.lower() in {'nan', 'none', ''}:        indication = ''    user_text = f'Indication: {indication}\n{_SYSTEM_PROMPT}' if indication else _SYSTEM_PROMPT    content = [{'type': 'image'}, {'type': 'text', 'text': user_text}]    prompt_text = spot_proc.apply_chat_template(        [{'role': 'user', 'content': content}],        add_generation_prompt=True, tokenize=False,    )    inputs = spot_proc(text=prompt_text, images=[pil_image], return_tensors='pt', padding=True)    inputs = {k: v.to(spot_model.device) for k, v in inputs.items()}    with torch.inference_mode():        out = spot_model.generate(            **inputs,            max_new_tokens=params['model']['max_new_tokens'],            do_sample=False,            pad_token_id=spot_proc.tokenizer.eos_token_id,        )    hyp = spot_proc.tokenizer.decode(        out[0][inputs['input_ids'].shape[1]:], skip_special_tokens=True    ).strip()    print(f'─── Sample {i+1} ───')    if indication:        print(f'INDICATION : {indication[:120]}')    print(f'REFERENCE  : {row["findings"].strip()[:300]}')    print(f'GENERATED  : {hyp[:300]}')    print()# Free VRAM before re-running other cellsdel spot_model, spot_proctorch.cuda.empty_cache()print('VRAM freed.')

import matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
matplotlib.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 120

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 4))

colors = {'uniform': '#1f77b4', 'weighted': '#d62728'}
for variant, color in colors.items():
    if variant not in results:
        continue
    log = results[variant].get('log_history', [])
    steps  = [e['step']  for e in log if 'loss' in e]
    losses = [e['loss']  for e in log if 'loss' in e]
    if steps:
        ax.plot(steps, losses, color=color, linewidth=1.5, label=f'sampler={variant}', alpha=0.9)

ax.set_xlabel('Training step')
ax.set_ylabel('Loss (causal LM)')
ax.set_title('QLoRA Training Loss — Uniform vs Weighted Sampler')
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
plt.tight_layout()

fig_path = FIGURES_DIR / 'train_loss_comparison.png'
fig.savefig(fig_path, dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
print(f'Saved {fig_path}')

Code
from src.data.labels import CHEXBERT_LABELS
from src.training.sampler import build_sample_weights

train_df = pd.read_parquet(PROCESSED_DIR / 'train.parquet')
train_df = train_df[train_df['findings'].notna() & (train_df['findings'].str.strip() != '')]
label_matrix = train_df[CHEXBERT_LABELS].values.astype(float)

p_target    = params['sampler'].get('p_target', {})
weight_clip = float(params['sampler'].get('weight_clip', 10.0))

weights_uniform  = build_sample_weights(label_matrix, p_target=None, weight_clip=weight_clip)
weights_weighted = build_sample_weights(label_matrix, p_target=p_target, weight_clip=weight_clip)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 4))

for ax, weights, title, color in [
    (axes[0], weights_uniform,  'Uniform sampler weights', '#1f77b4'),
    (axes[1], weights_weighted, 'Importance weights (weighted sampler)', '#d62728'),
]:
    w = np.asarray(weights, dtype=np.float64)
    w_min, w_max = w.min(), w.max()
    if w_min == w_max:
        w_min -= 0.5
        w_max += 0.5
    n_bins = max(1, min(60, len(np.unique(w))))
    bin_edges = np.linspace(w_min, w_max, n_bins + 1)
    ax.hist(w, bins=bin_edges, color=color, alpha=0.8, edgecolor='white', linewidth=0.3)
    ax.axvline(w.mean(), color='black', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5,
               label=f'mean={w.mean():.2f}')
    ax.axvline(np.median(w), color='red', linestyle=':', linewidth=1.5,
               label=f'median={np.median(w):.2f}')
    ax.set_xlabel('Sample weight')
    ax.set_ylabel('Training studies')
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.legend(fontsize=10)

ess = weights_weighted.sum()**2 / (weights_weighted**2).sum()
n   = len(weights_weighted)
axes[1].set_title(f'Importance weights — ESS={ess:.0f}/{n} ({100*ess/n:.1f}%)')

plt.tight_layout()
fig_path = FIGURES_DIR / 'train_sampler_weights_comparison.png'
fig.savefig(fig_path, dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
print(f'Saved {fig_path}')
plt.show()

title: 01 — EDA: Quantitative Distribution Audit
text:
STEP 6 — K_eff and Shannon entropy
Evaluate: how concentrated is the dataset? K_eff = 1 → one label dominates entirely. K_eff = 14 → all equal.

from src.eda.distribution_audit import compute_keff

keff = compute_keff(prevalences)
p = prevalences.values.clip(1e-9)
p_norm = p / p.sum()
entropy = float(-np.sum(p_norm * np.log(p_norm)))

print(f'Shannon entropy H  = {entropy:.3f} nats')
print(f'K_eff = exp(H)      = {keff:.2f}  (out of 14 possible)')
print()
print('Interpretation: the dataset behaves as if there are only')
print(f'{keff:.1f} equally-common labels — heavily skewed toward "No Finding".')

title: Evaluation Modules
text: This page documents the modules used to evaluate the model’s reports. It details the functions that compute standard natural language metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore) and clinical classification metrics (CheXbert). It also documents the code that calculates confidence calibration (how well the model’s confidence aligns with actual accuracy) and importance-weighted test evaluation.

title: Evaluation Modules
text:
Module: metrics.py
Located at src/eval/metrics.py.

Purpose
Implements the core metric evaluation stack. It handles natural language generation (NLG) overlap, semantic embedding matching, and clinical classification labelling.


Key Metrics Supported

CheXbert F1: Runs CheXbert on generated vs. reference text, generating 14-dimensional pathology vectors, and computes micro and macro F1 scores.
BERTScore: Computes contextual similarity using a DeBERTa language model (microsoft/deberta-xlarge-mnli). It is robust to vocabulary changes.
BLEU-4 / ROUGE-L: Measures token overlap and common subsequences, used primarily for comparison with existing literature.



Key Functions

compute_all_metrics(predictions: list[str], references: list[str], policy: UncertaintyPolicy) -> dict: Evaluates the generated findings against reference text using the full metric stack.
_patch_bert_score_overflow(): Workaround function that patches bert_score.utils.sent_encode to prevent tokenizer length conversion overflows.

title: Evaluation Modules
text:
Module: calibration.py
Located at src/eval/calibration.py.

Purpose
Measures the calibration gap between the generated report distribution and the reference reports.


Key Calculations

Prevalence Gap: The absolute difference between the prevalence of a pathology in the generated reports vs. the reference reports.
Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD): Measures divergence between the joint multi-label distributions of the generated and reference report datasets.



Key Functions

compute_prevalence_gaps(pred_matrix: np.ndarray, ref_matrix: np.ndarray) -> pd.Series: Computes absolute classification prevalence errors per clinical pathology.
evaluate_multilabel_calibration(pred_matrix: np.ndarray, ref_matrix: np.ndarray) -> dict: Returns the overall MMD metric and divergence statistics.

title: Evaluation Modules
text:
Module: shift_experiment.py
Located at src/eval/shift_experiment.py.

Purpose
Implements the importance-weighted test set evaluation to simulate different epidemiology mixes (prevalence shifts) without requiring distinct test sets.


Key Mathematical Formulations
When re-weighting the test set to represent a target prevalence, each study \(j\) is assigned a test weight \(v_j\). Weighted classification metrics use these weights to compute weighted precision and recall.
To verify the statistical stability of the weighted estimates, we calculate the Effective Sample Size (ESS):
\[ESS = \frac{\left( \sum_{j=1}^N v_j \right)^2}{\sum_{j=1}^N v_j^2}\]


Key Functions

compute_weighted_metrics(predictions: list[str], references: list[str], weights: np.ndarray) -> dict: Calculates weighted classification and BERTScore statistics.
run_prevalence_sweep(predictions: list[str], references: list[str], target_prevalences: list[dict]) -> pd.DataFrame: Sweeps across multiple target prevalence profiles, reporting performance degradation curves along with the corresponding ESS values.

title: Data Pipeline Modules
text: This page documents the code responsible for loading the raw chest X-ray reports, extracting 14 standard disease labels from free-text using a clinical language tool (CheXbert), and dividing the data into training, validation, and test splits. It ensures that multiple images belonging to the same patient are kept in the same split to prevent training leakage.

title: Data Pipeline Modules
text:
Module: load.py
Located at src/data/load.py.

Purpose
Loads the tabular CSV files from the raw dataset, merges them by study identifier (uid), collapses multi-projection images (frontal and lateral views) into grouped lists, and performs integrity checks.


Key Functions

load_reports(raw_dir: Path) -> pd.DataFrame: Reads indiana_reports.csv which contains study free-text columns (Findings, Impression, Indication, Problems).
load_projections(raw_dir: Path) -> pd.DataFrame: Reads indiana_projections.csv linking each study uid to image filenames and their perspective (Frontal or Lateral).
build_study_df(reports: pd.DataFrame, projections: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame: Performs a left-join grouping images by study. Filenames are aggregated into lists in columns frontal and lateral.
validate_integrity(df: pd.DataFrame) -> dict: Audits the merged DataFrame for missing reports or cases without frontal views. It outputs counts but does not drop entries.

title: Data Pipeline Modules
text:
Module: labels.py
Located at src/data/labels.py.

Purpose
Extracts 14 binary clinical labels from free-text report findings using the CheXbert classification model. It manages uncertain findings based on a versioned policy.


Uncertainty Policies

present: Uncertain findings (CheXbert output 3) are mapped to 1 (positive). This is the default policy.
absent: Uncertain findings and unmentioned findings are mapped to 0 (negative). This is used for ablation analysis.



Key Functions

extract_labels_batch(texts: list[str], policy: UncertaintyPolicy) -> np.ndarray: Processes list of findings using CheXbert, returning an \((n, 14)\) binary numpy matrix. It uses token-level batching for high throughput.
_raw_preds_to_binary(preds: np.ndarray, policy: UncertaintyPolicy) -> np.ndarray: Converts the four CheXbert classes (0=blank, 1=positive, 2=negative, 3=uncertain) into binary outputs based on the policy.

title: Data Pipeline Modules
text:
Module: split.py
Located at src/data/split.py.

Purpose
Divides the study cohort into training, validation, and test sets. It prevents patient-level data leakage and preserves the label distributions.


Design Decisions

Study-Level Splitting: Patients can have multiple images in a study. Splitting is done by study uid so that both frontal and lateral views for a study are kept in the same split.
Iterative Multi-label Stratification: We use iterative_train_test_split from scikit-multilearn to ensure the percentage of rare pathologies remains consistent across training, validation, and testing.



Key Functions

split_dataset(df: pd.DataFrame, test_size: float, val_size: float, random_state: int, min_test_count: int) -> Tuple[pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame]: Orchestrates the stratification. Ensures the test set has at least min_test_count studies (default 600).
_iterative_split(df: pd.DataFrame, label_matrix: np.ndarray, test_fraction: float, random_state: int) -> Tuple[pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame]: Wrapper function for the scikit-multilearn algorithm.

title: 6. Production Scaling
text: This section looks at what happens when we move from research to production. We discuss the speed (latency) and cost of running a 4-billion parameter model in real-time, explain how we can build safety systems that let the AI say “I don’t know” when its confidence is low, and introduce advanced medical metrics that evaluate clinical errors (such as swapping left and right) rather than just looking at sentence similarity.

title: 6. Production Scaling
text:
Advanced Clinical Metrics
While BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore measure token overlap and semantic similarity, they do not guarantee clinical accuracy. In production, we propose evaluating:

GREEN (LLM-as-a-Judge): A GPT-4 based metric trained to identify and categorize clinically significant errors (e.g., misidentifying lateralities, missing critical findings, or hallucinating devices).
RadCliQ (Radiology Report Clinical Quality): A metric that combines lexical overlap and clinical entity matching, calibrated directly to match human radiologist error ratings.
RaTEScore: A semantic metric specifically designed for medical report generation, showing robust handling of clinical synonyms and negation detection.

title: 6. Production Scaling
text:
Production Serving Considerations
Serving a 4B parameter model in real-time clinical workflows requires careful infrastructure design:

Latency: Average token generation for 256 tokens on a modern GPU takes ~1-3 seconds. For real-time radiologist dictation assistance, streaming generation tokens via WebSockets is required.
Abstention & Selective Prediction: A clinical model must know when to say “I don’t know.” We propose implementing an abstention threshold based on output token entropy. If the model’s confidence falls below a threshold, the study is automatically escalated to a senior radiologist without AI output.
OOD (Out-of-Distribution) Guardrails: A production pipeline should run our DomainShiftAudit tests on a small batch of incoming hospital data weekly. If the computed image noise or disease prevalence shifts drastically, the system flags a warning to the MLOps team.
Real OOD Validation: A single dataset can never prove generalization. We discuss why validating on a second independent clinical dataset from a separate hospital system is a mandatory prerequisite for clinical deployment.

title: 4. Evaluation & Metric Pivot
text: This section highlights our most important scientific finding. Initially, our automated grader (CheXbert) showed that the model’s pathology scores collapsed to zero after training. However, by reading the actual reports generated by the AI, we discovered that the AI was performing excellently. It was describing pathologies in clinical terms (e.g., “enlarged heart”) instead of shorthand terms (e.g., “cardiomegaly”). CheXbert was too rigid to match these, leading us to pivot to BERTScore, which evaluates semantic meaning rather than literal vocabulary.

title: 4. Evaluation & Metric Pivot
text:
Baseline Zero-Shot Evaluation
Before training, we establish the performance of the raw google/medgemma-4b-it model on the 600-study test set under zero-shot prompting. This represents our baseline performance.
Zero-Shot Baseline Results (reports/baseline_results.json): * BERTScore-F1: \(0.6938\) * BLEU-4: \(0.0957\) * ROUGE-L: \(0.2635\) * CheXbert micro-F1 (U\(\rightarrow\)present): \(0.3967\) * CheXbert macro-F1 (U\(\rightarrow\)present): \(0.1416\)

title: 4. Evaluation & Metric Pivot
text:
The Metric Pivot: CheXbert \(\rightarrow\) BERTScore
During Phase 4 (v1 and v2 training), we encountered a critical anomaly that led to a major architectural pivot.

The Macro-F1 Collapse Anomaly
In v1 and v2 training, we optimized the checkpoint selection callback to save the model that achieved the highest CheXbert micro-F1. However, after fine-tuning, the macro-F1 collapsed by ~57% (dropping from \(0.1416\) zero-shot to \(0.0400\) fine-tuned), even though training loss showed a healthy monotonic decay.
We formulated two competing hypotheses: * H1 (Training Collapse): The model suffered from catastrophic forgetting and learned to output a static “normal” report for all cases. * H2 (Metric Mismatch / Vocabulary Shift): The model is generating clinically accurate findings but using local IU X-ray vocabulary that the CheXbert labeler (which was trained on Boston-based MIMIC-CXR reports) cannot parse.


Verification via Spot-Check
We loaded the fine-tuned adapter and ran qualitative generations on validation samples (Notebook 03, STEP 7b). The results confirmed H2:









Case
Ground Truth Pathologies
Model Generated Report
CheXbert Evaluation




Case 3
Emphysema, LLL airspace disease
“Hyperexpanded lungs with flattened diaphragms, opacification in the left lower lobe”
No Finding (Failure)


Case 4
Cardiomegaly, Congestion
“Enlarged cardiac silhouette, bilateral airspace disease, bilateral pleural effusions”
No Finding (Failure)



CheXbert was trained on specific MIMIC-CXR terms (e.g., “emphysema,” “cardiomegaly”). IU X-ray reports (which the model learned to emulate) describe these findings descriptively (e.g., “hyperexpanded lungs with flattened diaphragms” for emphysema; “enlarged cardiac silhouette” for cardiomegaly). Because CheXbert looks for rigid clinical keyword mappings, it labeled the model’s highly accurate descriptive reports as “No Finding”, dragging the classification macro-F1 to zero.


The Pivot: BERTScore-F1 Checkpointing
To resolve this, we pivoted to BERTScore-F1 (using microsoft/deberta-xlarge-mnli embeddings) as our primary checkpoint selection metric. BERTScore measures semantic contextual similarity. Because “hyperexpanded lungs” and “emphysema” map to the same clinical semantic space, BERTScore correctly rewards the model for accurate descriptions regardless of exact vocabulary. CheXbert F1 was demoted to a diagnostic metric only.

Results Analysis

Semantic Improvement: Both fine-tuned models outperform the zero-shot base model on BERTScore-F1 (as shown in Figure 1 (b)), indicating that fine-tuning succeeded in aligning MedGemma to the reporting style of the target dataset.
Uniform vs. Weighted: The uniform sampler slightly outperformed the weighted sampler (0.7113 vs 0.7042). In this run, the weighted sampler used null target prevalences (acting as a uniform distribution with a different seed).
Early Convergence: The model peaked at Epoch 1 and began to regress on Epoch 2 (see the loss decay in Figure 1 (a)). This suggests that the training set is small enough (~2,700 studies) that the adaptation saturates quickly, or that a learning rate of \(5 \times 10^{-5}\) is too aggressive, leading to overfitting to the training vocabulary.
Sampler Verification: The distribution of sampling weights in Figure 1 (c) confirms that active weight clipping functions correctly, preventing single outlier studies with multiple pathologies from dominating the batch selection process.

title: 2. Data Distribution & Sampler Correction
text: This section explains how we handle the huge imbalance in clinical data. Most X-rays are completely normal, meaning that an AI trained on this data would quickly learn to default to predicting “normal” to get easy high scores. We solve this by calculating mathematical weights for each study. When the AI trains, it is shown rare pathologies more frequently and normal X-rays less frequently. This forces the model to actually learn the signatures of rare diseases rather than taking lazy shortcuts.

title: 2. Data Distribution & Sampler Correction
text:
Dataset Distribution
The Indiana University Chest X-ray (IU CXR) dataset is heavily skewed toward normal findings. Out of ~3,300 training studies, ~56% contain “No Finding.” Rare diseases (such as Pneumothorax or Consolidation) account for less than 1% of the dataset.











(a) Label Prevalences in the IU CXR Dataset











(b) Pathology Co-occurrence Matrix













(c) Effective Sample Size (ESS) per Clinical Label







Figure 1: Clinical data distributions and label statistics.



The severe class imbalance is illustrated in Figure 1 (a) and Figure 1 (b). To further quantify the representation of rare pathologies, we compute the Effective Sample Size (ESS) per class (see Figure 1 (c)).

title: 2. Data Distribution & Sampler Correction
text:
Importance Sampling & Sampler Correction
To prevent the model from collapsing to the majority class and simply predicting “normal” for all patients, we implement a WeightedRandomSampler. For each study \(j\), we compute an importance weight \(w_j\) based on the target prevalences specified in params.yaml:
\[w_j = \text{clip}\left( \left( \prod_{l \in L_j} \frac{p_{\text{target}}(l)}{p_{\text{dataset}}(l)} \right)^{\frac{1}{|L_j|}}, \text{clip\_val} \right)\]
Where: * \(L_j\) is the set of positive pathologies present in study \(j\). * \(p_{\text{target}}(l)\) is the user-specified target prevalence for pathology \(l\). * \(p_{\text{dataset}}(l)\) is the empirical prevalence of pathology \(l\) in the training set. * \(\text{clip\_val}\) is the clipping multiplier (sampler.weight_clip = 10.0) to avoid high-variance gradients from extremely rare combinations.
For studies with no positive pathologies (“No Finding”), we assign a base weight:
\[w_j = \frac{p_{\text{target}}(\text{No Finding})}{p_{\text{dataset}}(\text{No Finding})}\]
Applying these weights at the batch-selection level forces the model to encounter rare pathology patterns during backpropagation without modifying the loss function, avoiding gradient-scale instabilities.

title: ReportCXR
text:
This project is an advanced AI system built for automated chest X-ray interpretation. Instead of simply fine-tuning a general-purpose model, this work addresses a critical, real-world challenge in healthcare AI: domain shift. Medical AI models trained on United States clinical datasets (which are predominantly English and heavily biased towards normal findings) often fail when deployed in Latin American (LATAM) hospitals due to differences in scanner equipment, local patient populations (prevalences of disease), and local reporting languages.
Here, we build a robust, reproducible clinical language model based on Google’s MedGemma 4B-it, construct a data pipeline using DVC (Data Version Control) and Weights & Biases (W&B) for full MLOps auditing, and establish a Domain Shift Audit Protocol to systematically test and quantify model degradation under simulated equipment, language, and epidemiological shifts.

title: ReportCXR
text:
Project Overview
The ReportCXR project is developed as part of the Eden AI technical challenge for the Applied Research role. The system fine-tunes a medical vision-language model, google/medgemma-4b-it (which combines a frozen medical SigLIP image encoder and a Gemma 3 4B-it language decoder), to generate radiological reports (specifically the Findings section) conditioned on Clinical Indications.

The Strategic Thesis: Domain Shift Auditing
Our central premise is that deploying medical models across geographies is a distributional shift problem. Rather than reporting static accuracy metrics on a clean dataset, this project designs and validates a reusable diagnostic infrastructure—the DomainShiftAudit framework—on simulated shifts inside the Indiana University Chest X-ray (IU CXR) dataset.
This protocol acts as a diagnostic suite. It measures the robustness of models before they go live: 1. Acquisition Shift: Simulates older equipment or different scanner manufacturers by applying synthetic noise, contrast adjustments, and compression. 2. Language Shift: Assesses how a model trained in English performs when prompting and generating in Spanish. 3. Prevalence Shift: Simulates different disease rates using importance sampling to evaluate how the model handles a LATAM patient distribution.

title: ReportCXR
text:
Website Structure
Use the navigation menu at the top to explore the work:

Technical Report: A modular, multi-part deep dive into the strategic thesis, data distribution, model training, evaluation metrics, domain shift experiments, and production scaling.
Code Documentation: Detailed module-by-module documentation of every component in the codebase (data loading, label extraction, QLoRA training, metric evaluation, and synthetic shifts).
Notebooks: Step-by-step interactive implementations of our entire pipeline (EDA, baseline zero-shot, training, and evaluation).

title: ReportCXR
text:
MLOps Setup and Reproducibility
This project is built for complete mathematical reproducibility. Data, parameters, and pipelines are versioned.

1. Versioned Parameters (params.yaml)
Every parameter—from QLoRA rank to target sampler disease prevalences—is declared in a single source of truth: params.yaml. No thresholds or weights are hardcoded inside the code in src/.


2. DVC Pipeline (dvc.yaml)
The data pipeline is managed via DVC (Data Version Control). To reproduce the pipeline stages, run:
# Verify status of data and steps
dvc status

# Reproduce the entire pipeline (loading -> labeling -> splitting -> training -> evaluation)
dvc repro
The pipeline contains the following stages defined in dvc.yaml: * load: Joins reports and projections CSVs and links images. * labels: Processes free-text findings with a CheXbert labeler to build a 14-dimensional pathology matrix. * split: Employs iterative multi-label stratification to split studies cleanly without patient leakage. * eda: Computes prevalences and class statistics, outputting training distribution plots. * train_uniform: Trains QLoRA on a uniform distribution. * train_weighted: Trains QLoRA with active sampling weights. * eval: Computes test metrics on the best saved models.


3. Experiment Tracking (Weights and Biases)
All training runs, validation curves, and sampler weight distributions are synced to Weights and Biases under the project reportcxr.

title: 1. Introduction & Strategic Thesis
text: This section introduces the core clinical motivation of the project. Chest X-ray models are usually trained on massive datasets from large US hospitals, which contain mostly English reports and a very high percentage of normal X-rays. When trying to deploy these models in Latin American (LATAM) clinics, they face three major changes: different image properties from different X-ray machines, different languages (Spanish instead of English), and different rates of disease. We frame this problem not as a simple translation or accuracy task, but as a systematic domain shift diagnostic challenge.

title: 1. Introduction & Strategic Thesis
text:
Clinical Domain Shift
The Eden AI technical challenge focuses on developing a multimodal medical vision-language foundation model that generates chest X-ray findings. However, deploying such a model in a real clinical setting—particularly across distinct geographic regions like Latin America—introduces severe out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts:

Acquisition Shift: Differences in scanner hardware manufacturers, kVp exposure calibrations, and sensor resolutions produce raw image distributions that diverge from the clean datasets used during pre-training.
Prevalence Shift: Regional epidemiological patterns result in different baseline frequencies of pathologies. For example, Latin American public clinics may exhibit higher prevalences of Tuberculosis or Chagas disease compared to standard Western benchmarks.
Language Shift: Pre-trained clinical vision-language models like MedGemma are pre-trained predominantly on English corpora (e.g., MIMIC-CXR). Clinicians in LATAM dictate findings and indications in Spanish.

title: 1. Introduction & Strategic Thesis
text:
The Strategic Thesis
Our thesis is that cross-geographic deployment must be framed as a distributional discrepancy audit. Rather than optimizing a model to achieve maximum accuracy on a single dataset, we construct a reusable diagnostic infrastructure—the DomainShiftAudit class—capable of auditing models under simulated shifts.
By validating this diagnostic protocol on controlled, synthetic perturbations within the Indiana University Chest X-ray (IU CXR) dataset, we ensure that the auditing infrastructure is fully developed and ready to be run on real, local clinical data from day one.

title: 3. Model & QLoRA Config
text: This section describes the AI model itself and how we train it. We use Google’s MedGemma, which consists of a frozen medical eye (image encoder) and a text generator (language decoder). We train only the text generator using a method called QLoRA, which inserts tiny adjustable elements into the model while keeping the main text book frozen in memory. We also use a masking technique to ensure the model is only graded on the clinical findings it generates, not on the inputs it was given.

title: 3. Model & QLoRA Config
text:
MedGemma Foundation Architecture
We utilize google/medgemma-4b-it as our base model. This foundation architecture is composed of: 1. Vision Encoder: A frozen medical SigLIP model that extracts visual representation vectors directly from chest radiograph projections. 2. Language Decoder: A Gemma 3 4B instruction-tuned causal language model, which receives concatenated vision tokens and clinical text prompts.
graph LR
    CXR[CXR Image] --> SigLIP[SigLIP Encoder (Frozen)]
    Prompt[Clinical Indication Prompt] --> Tokenizer[Tokenizer]
    SigLIP --> Concat[Concat Tokens]
    Tokenizer --> Concat
    Concat --> Gemma[Gemma 3 4B Decoder (QLoRA)]
    Gemma --> Report[Generated Findings]

title: 3. Model & QLoRA Config
text:
Low-Rank Adaptation (QLoRA)
To train the model on a consumer GPU with limited VRAM (21 GB Ada RTX 4000), we employ QLoRA (Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation). The base model weights are loaded in 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4) double quantization, while low-rank trainable adapter matrices are injected into the attention projection layers.
Mathematically, the forward pass for a projection layer with input \(x \in \mathbb{R}^d\) is given by:
\[h = W_0 x + \Delta W x = W_0 x + \frac{s}{r} B A x\]
Where: * \(W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}\) represents the frozen base model weights in NF4 representation. * \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}\) and \(B \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times r}\) are the trainable LoRA adapter matrices. * \(r\) is the LoRA rank (\(r = 16\)). * \(s\) is the scaling factor (\(\alpha = 32\), yielding \(\frac{s}{r} = 2.0\)).
The adapters are applied to the attention modules: \(q\_proj\), \(k\_proj\), \(v\_proj\), and \(o\_proj\). This reduces the trainable parameters to just ~17M (~0.4%) of the 4B parameter model. The vision encoder is kept fully frozen (\(requires\_grad=False\)) to prevent overfitting on the relatively small dataset.

title: 3. Model & QLoRA Config
3. Model & QLoRA Config
text:
Training Arguments
Key training hyperparameters (params.yaml → training, v3): * learning_rate: \(5 \times 10^{-5}\) * num_epochs: 2 * warmup_ratio: 0.06 * batch_size: 2 * gradient_accumulation_steps: 8 (effective batch size = 16) * max_seq_length: 512 * optim: paged_adamw_8bit

title: 5. Domain Shift Audit Protocol
text: This section outlines our system stress-testing infrastructure. Rather than hoping the model works when moved to a new hospital, we write a diagnostic program that simulates three types of shifts: scanner machinery changes (adding noise and changing brightness/contrast), language transitions (evaluating Spanish generations), and epidemiological changes (simulating different rates of diseases). We leave placeholders for the exact degradation curves, as this represents our testing protocol.

title: 5. Domain Shift Audit Protocol
text:
Overview of the Audit Protocol
To evaluate the system’s readiness for Latin American clinical environments, we construct the DomainShiftAudit suite. This module simulates the three core axes of distributional shift.

title: 5. Domain Shift Audit Protocol
text:
Axis A: Acquisition & Equipment Shift
We apply controlled synthetic image perturbations to simulate differences in scanner hardware, sensor noise, or digitization quality (e.g., older equipment in LATAM public hospitals). We sweep over: * Gaussian Noise: Adding noise with standard deviation \(\sigma \in [0, 50]\) in pixel units. * Contrast Scaling: Factors \(c \in [0.5, 2.0]\). * Gamma Correction: \(\gamma \in [0.5, 2.0]\) (simulating exposure discrepancies). * JPEG Compression: Qualities \(q \in [95, 75, 50, 25, 10]\) (simulating network bandwith saving compression).
Space left for remaining experimental degradation curves.

title: 5. Domain Shift Audit Protocol
text:
Axis B: Language Shift
Since MedGemma was pre-trained primarily on English medical text, deploying it in a Spanish-speaking hospital represents a severe vocabulary shift. We test the model’s Spanish generation by prompting it to output findings in Spanish and evaluate the output against automatic Spanish translations of the ground-truth findings.
Space left for remaining language degradation metrics.

title: 5. Domain Shift Audit Protocol
text:
Axis C: Prevalence Shift
Medical centers in LATAM exhibit different prevalence distributions of diseases compared to US datasets (e.g., higher rates of Tuberculosis or Chagas). We simulate this shift by re-weighting our test set using importance sampling. We compute weighted test metrics and report the Effective Sample Size (ESS) to ensure our estimators remain statistically stable:
\[ESS = \frac{\left( \sum_{j=1}^N w_j \right)^2}{\sum_{j=1}^N w_j^2}\]
If the \(ESS\) falls below a safety threshold (e.g., \(ESS < 50\)), we flag the evaluation as statistically unreliable due to extreme distribution divergence.
Space left for remaining importance-weighted test curves.

title: Code Architecture Overview
text: This page describes the organization of the code repository. We explain what each folder does, show how data flows through our modular Python files, and discuss why we avoid writing all of our logic in single, massive Jupyter Notebooks. Instead, we write clean, reusable Python scripts, using notebooks only to orchestrate and visualize results.

title: Code Architecture Overview
text:
Directory Structure
The codebase is organized into modules to ensure a strict separation of concerns. All core logic resides in src/, while notebooks are strictly reserved for orchestration and visualization:
ReportCXR/
│
├── params.yaml                    # Single source of truth for all parameters
├── dvc.yaml                       # Pipeline stages and dependency tracking
├── pyproject.toml                 # Package configuration
│
├── src/
│   ├── data/                      # Data downloading, processing, splitting
│   ├── eda/                       # Distribution auditing
│   ├── training/                  # Model loading, sampling, training loops
│   ├── eval/                      # Metrics and evaluation scripts
│   └── domain_shift_audit/        # Stress-testing infrastructure
│
├── notebooks/                     # Orchestration notebooks
└── reports/                       # Generated figures, logs, baseline JSONs

title: Code Architecture Overview
text:
Modular Design Principles

Logic in Python Modules: We avoid monolithic notebooks. Scripts in src/ are imported into notebooks. This allows for unit testing, easier git diffing, and cleaner refactoring.
Versioned Configuration: All numerical constants, paths, and hyperparameters are housed in params.yaml. Scripts read parameters at runtime, preventing silent configuration drift.
Pipeline Reproducibility: The dvc.yaml file defines explicit data lineage. This ensures that change in code or parameter will trigger only the necessary downstream stages.
Experiment Tracking: The HuggingFace trainer logs to Weights & Biases automatically. This logs the complete history of training losses, learning rates, validation BERTScore, and CheXbert classification F1.

title: Training Pipeline Modules
text: This page documents the components used to configure, sample, and train the AI model. We explain the modules that load the MedGemma weights in a compressed format, compute the study sampling weights for balancing, build training prompts, and run the training loop. It also documents the callback that evaluates the model after every epoch and saves the best version based on semantic score.

title: Training Pipeline Modules
text:
Module: model.py
Located at src/training/model.py.

Purpose
Handles loading google/medgemma-4b-it from Hugging Face with 4-bit NF4 quantization, sets up the Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) LoRA configurations, and constructs prompt structures.


Key Functions

load_model_and_processor(model_id: str, quantization: str, device_map: str) -> Tuple[Any, Any]: Loads the model and the associated image/text processor. Initializes BitsAndBytesConfig for 4-bit NF4 representation.
apply_qlora(model: Any, lora_config: dict) -> Any: Injects trainable LoRA matrices into attention projections (q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj) with configured rank, alpha, and dropout.
build_prompt(indication: str) -> str: Formats clinical context into MedGemma chat template format.
_best_attn_impl() -> str: Selects the optimal attention implementation (flash_attention_2, sdpa, or eager) depending on local package support.

title: Training Pipeline Modules
text:
Module: sampler.py
Located at src/training/sampler.py.

Purpose
Calculates per-study training weights based on target prevalences and instantiates WeightedRandomSampler instances.


Key Functions

build_sample_weights(label_matrix: np.ndarray, p_target: dict, weight_clip: float) -> np.ndarray: Computes the geometric mean of importance ratios for positive labels on each study:
\[w_j = \left( \prod_{l \in L_j} \frac{p_{\text{target}}(l)}{p_{\text{dataset}}(l)} \right)^{\frac{1}{|L_j|}}\]
Weights are clipped at weight_clip × mean_weight to prevent high gradient variance.
build_sampler(weights: np.ndarray) -> WeightedRandomSampler: Returns a PyTorch sampler that selects batch items based on the calculated weight distribution.

title: Training Pipeline Modules
text:
Module: train.py
Located at src/training/train.py.

Purpose
Orchestrates the SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning) loop using the HuggingFace Trainer interface. It manages datasets, prompt collators, and checkpoint callbacks.


Key Classes & Functions

CXRReportDataset(Dataset): PyTorch dataset. Loads parquet split data and pairs each study with its corresponding chest X-ray image.
make_collate_fn(processor: Any, max_len: int) -> Callable: Collator function that tokenizes the text prompt and full generation, builds the completion mask by setting label indices before the Findings token length to -100, and handles fallback blank gray images when frontal images are missing.
F1CheckpointCallback(TrainerCallback): Runs greedy validation text generation at the end of each training epoch, computes DeBERTa BERTScore-F1 and CheXbert classification scores, logs metrics to Weights & Biases, and saves the adapter checkpoint if BERTScore improves.
CXRTrainer(Trainer): Extension of HuggingFace Trainer to inject the custom WeightedRandomSampler into the training dataloader.
plot_training_figures(...): Automatically plots training loss decay, validation metric curves, and sampler weight distributions.

title: Domain Shift Audit Modules
text: This page documents the Stress-Testing and Audit suite. The codebase packages individual stress-tests (scanner noise, Spanish translation evaluation, and epidemiological shifts) under a single, unified class called DomainShiftAudit. This class acts as a reusable tool. Instead of running separate experiments, developers can run this audit tool on any model-data pair to analyze degradation behavior before deployment.

title: Domain Shift Audit Modules
text:
Module: audit.py
Located at src/domain_shift_audit/audit.py.

Purpose
Acts as the central orchestrator for the domain shift audit. It unifies the three sub-experiments under a common interface:
auditor = DomainShiftAudit(model, processor)
result = auditor.audit(dataset, images, shift_type='acquisition', perturb_type='gaussian_noise')


Key Classes

ShiftAuditResult: Dataclass container returning:

shift_type: Axis probed (acquisition, language, or prevalence).
degradation_curve: DataFrame containing columns [magnitude, metric, relative_degradation].
ess: Effective Sample Size series (populated for prevalence sweeps).
ci_low / ci_high: Bootstrap confidence intervals.

DomainShiftAudit: The runner class. Orchestrates the pipeline and delegates execution to specialized shift modules depending on the configuration.

title: Domain Shift Audit Modules
text:
Module: acquisition_shift.py
Located at src/domain_shift_audit/acquisition_shift.py.

Purpose
Simulates scanner-level perturbations on input images to evaluate visual encoder robustness under hardware discrepancies.


Supported Perturbations

perturb_brightness(img, factor): Scales pixel intensities.
perturb_contrast(img, factor): Adjusts contrast scaling.
perturb_gamma(img, gamma): Power-law correction \(I_{out} = (I_{in}/255)^{\gamma} \times 255\).
perturb_gaussian_noise(img, std): Adds zero-mean Gaussian noise.
perturb_jpeg_compression(img, quality): Performs lossy compression.



Key Functions

run_acquisition_sweep(images: list, perturb_type: PerturbType, magnitudes: list, metric_fn: Callable) -> AcquisitionSweepResult: Iterates over magnitudes, applies image transforms, runs the metric evaluation function, and compiles the degradation curve.

title: Domain Shift Audit Modules
text:
Module: language_shift.py
Located at src/domain_shift_audit/language_shift.py.

Purpose
Evaluates MedGemma’s generation when prompting and outputting reports in Spanish.


Key Functions

run_language_shift(model: Any, processor: Any, dataset: pd.DataFrame, target_language: str) -> dict: Evaluates predictions generated in Spanish against translated reference findings. It reports BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore variations relative to the English baseline.

title: Domain Shift Audit Modules
text:
Module: prevalence_shift.py
Located at src/domain_shift_audit/prevalence_shift.py.

Purpose
Re-envisions the importance-weighted test set sweep as an audit of model robustness under regional epidemiological changes (e.g., target LATAM clinical environments).


Key Functions

run_prevalence_audit(predictions: list, references: list, label_matrix: np.ndarray, target_scenarios: list[dict]) -> dict: Evaluates model degradation across clinical scenarios, matching predictions against simulated target disease prevalences, and tracking ESS.

title: 02 — Baseline: MedGemma 4B-it Zero-Shot
text:
STEP 3 — Zero-shot generation

Each test study → frontal CXR image + indication → generated Findings text
Temperature = 0 (greedy, do_sample=False)
Hypotheses saved to data/processed/zero_shot_hypotheses.txt — skip inference on re-run

Evaluate: do the generated reports look clinically plausible?


Code
from PIL import Image

SYSTEM_PROMPT = (
    'You are an expert radiologist. '
    'Write only the Findings section of a radiology report for the chest X-ray shown. '
    'Be concise and clinical. Do not include an Impression section.'
)


def _build_messages(indication: str | None, has_image: bool) -> list:
    content = []
    if has_image:
        content.append({'type': 'image'})
    user_text = SYSTEM_PROMPT
    if indication and indication.strip() and indication.strip().lower() not in {'nan', 'none'}:
        user_text = f'Indication: {indication.strip()}\n{SYSTEM_PROMPT}'
    content.append({'type': 'text', 'text': user_text})
    return [{'role': 'user', 'content': content}]


@torch.inference_mode()
def generate_report(row: pd.Series) -> str:
    """Generate a zero-shot findings report for one test study."""
    # Load frontal image
    pil_image = None
    frontal_files = row.get('frontal', [])
    if isinstance(frontal_files, list) and frontal_files:
        img_path = IMAGES_DIR / frontal_files[0]
        if img_path.exists():
            try:
                pil_image = Image.open(img_path).convert('RGB')
            except Exception:
                pil_image = None

    indication = str(row.get('indication', '')) if pd.notna(row.get('indication')) else ''
    messages   = _build_messages(indication, has_image=pil_image is not None)

    # Tokenize via chat template
    text = processor.apply_chat_template(messages, add_generation_prompt=True, tokenize=False)
    proc_kwargs = dict(text=text, return_tensors='pt', padding=True)
    if pil_image is not None:
        proc_kwargs['images'] = [pil_image]
    inputs = processor(**proc_kwargs)
    inputs = {k: v.to(model.device) for k, v in inputs.items()}

    input_len = inputs['input_ids'].shape[1]
    outputs   = model.generate(
        **inputs,
        max_new_tokens=params['model']['max_new_tokens'],
        do_sample=False,           # greedy = temperature 0
        pad_token_id=processor.tokenizer.eos_token_id,
    )
    new_tokens = outputs[0][input_len:]
    return processor.tokenizer.decode(new_tokens, skip_special_tokens=True).strip()


print('generate_report() defined. Ready to run inference.')


generate_report() defined. Ready to run inference.

from tqdm.auto import tqdm

if HYP_FILE.exists():
    print(f'Loading cached hypotheses from {HYP_FILE}')
    hypotheses = HYP_FILE.read_text().splitlines()
    assert len(hypotheses) == len(test_df), (
        f'Cache mismatch: {len(hypotheses)} lines vs {len(test_df)} test studies. '
        f'Delete {HYP_FILE} and re-run to regenerate.'
    )
    print(f'Loaded {len(hypotheses)} hypotheses.')
else:
    print(f'Running zero-shot inference on {len(test_df)} studies ...')
    hypotheses = []
    for _, row in tqdm(test_df.iterrows(), total=len(test_df), desc='generating'):
        hyp = generate_report(row)
        # Newlines break the line-per-study format — collapse to space
        hypotheses.append(hyp.replace('\n', ' '))

    HYP_FILE.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    HYP_FILE.write_text('\n'.join(hypotheses))
    print(f'Saved {len(hypotheses)} hypotheses to {HYP_FILE}')

references = test_df['findings'].str.strip().str.replace('\n', ' ', regex=False).tolist()
print(f'\nHypotheses: {len(hypotheses)}  |  References: {len(references)}')

text:
STEP 4 — CheXbert labeling
Run CheXbert over both hypotheses and references under both uncertainty policies.
Cached to parquet after first run (each pass takes ~5–10 min on CPU, <1 min on GPU).
Evaluate: do hypothesis label distributions look reasonable compared to references?


Code
from src.data.labels import run_chexbert

CHEXBERT_DEVICE = DEVICE  # use GPU if available — much faster

# Policy = 'present'  (uncertain → 1; default handout policy)
HYP_PRESENT_CACHE = PROCESSED_DIR / 'baseline_hyp_labels_present.parquet'
REF_PRESENT_CACHE = PROCESSED_DIR / 'baseline_ref_labels_present.parquet'

def _load_or_compute_labels(texts, policy, cache_path, label_name):
    if cache_path.exists():
        print(f'  Loading {label_name} (policy={policy}) from cache ...')
        return pd.read_parquet(cache_path).to_numpy(dtype=np.int8)
    print(f'  Computing {label_name} labels (policy={policy}) ...')
    mat = run_chexbert(texts, uncertain_policy=policy, device=CHEXBERT_DEVICE)
    df_cache = pd.DataFrame(mat, columns=CHEXBERT_LABELS)
    df_cache.to_parquet(cache_path, index=False)
    print(f'  Cached to {cache_path}')
    return mat


print('Policy = present (uncertain → 1):')
hyp_mat_present = _load_or_compute_labels(hypotheses, 'present', HYP_PRESENT_CACHE, 'hyp')
ref_mat_present = _load_or_compute_labels(references, 'present', REF_PRESENT_CACHE, 'ref')

print('\nLabel prevalence comparison (policy=present):')
prev_ref  = pd.Series(ref_mat_present.mean(axis=0), index=CHEXBERT_LABELS, name='reference')
prev_hyp  = pd.Series(hyp_mat_present.mean(axis=0), index=CHEXBERT_LABELS, name='hypothesis')
prev_comp = pd.concat([prev_ref, prev_hyp], axis=1)
prev_comp['diff'] = prev_hyp - prev_ref
display(prev_comp.sort_values('reference', ascending=False).round(3))

title: 02 — Baseline: MedGemma 4B-it Zero-Shot
text:
STEP 5 — F1-CheXbert
Compute micro-average F1, macro-average F1, and per-label F1 using sklearn’s classification_report on the CheXbert label matrices.
Both policies are computed; present is the primary metric.
Evaluate: which labels does zero-shot MedGemma fail on?


Code
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report


def _compute_f1_chexbert(ref_mat, hyp_mat, policy_name: str) -> dict:
    cr = classification_report(
        ref_mat, hyp_mat,
        target_names=CHEXBERT_LABELS,
        output_dict=True,
        zero_division=0,
    )
    per_label = {label: cr[label]['f1-score'] for label in CHEXBERT_LABELS}
    return {
        f'f1_chexbert_micro_{policy_name}': cr['micro avg']['f1-score'],
        f'f1_chexbert_macro_{policy_name}': cr['macro avg']['f1-score'],
        f'per_label_f1_{policy_name}': per_label,
        f'_cr_{policy_name}': cr,
    }


f1_present = _compute_f1_chexbert(ref_mat_present, hyp_mat_present, 'present')
f1_absent  = _compute_f1_chexbert(ref_mat_absent,  hyp_mat_absent,  'absent')

print('F1-CheXbert (policy = present):')

print(f"  Macro F1 : {f1_present['f1_chexbert_macro_present']:.4f}")

title: 02 — Baseline: MedGemma 4B-it Zero-Shot
text:
STEP 6 — BERTScore
Contextual similarity using DeBERTa-XL-MNLI as the reference model.
Returns per-study F1 scores (useful for distribution plots).
Evaluate: mean BERTScore F1 — is the model generating plausible clinical language?


Code
import bert_score.utils as _bsu
from bert_score import score as bert_score_fn

# deberta-xlarge-mnli stores model_max_length ≈ 1e30 in its config.
# bert_score.utils.sent_encode passes that directly to tokenizer.encode(max_length=...),
# which overflows the Rust tokenizer backend even with use_fast_tokenizer=False
# (deberta-v2 has no slow tokenizer). Patch it to cap at 512.
_orig_sent_encode = _bsu.sent_encode

def _patched_sent_encode(tokenizer, sent):
    return tokenizer.encode(sent, add_special_tokens=True, max_length=512, truncation=True)

_bsu.sent_encode = _patched_sent_encode

BERTSCORE_MODEL = params['eval']['bertscore_model']  # microsoft/deberta-xlarge-mnli

print(f'Computing BERTScore with {BERTSCORE_MODEL} ...')
bp, br, bf = bert_score_fn(
    hypotheses,
    references,
    model_type=BERTSCORE_MODEL,
    device=DEVICE,
    verbose=True,
)

_bsu.sent_encode = _orig_sent_encode  # restore after use

bertscore_f1        = float(bf.mean())
bertscore_per_study = bf.numpy()  # shape (n,)

print(f'\nBERTScore — precision: {float(bp.mean()):.4f} | recall: {float(br.mean()):.4f} | F1: {bertscore_f1:.4f}')

title: 02 — Baseline: MedGemma 4B-it Zero-Shot
text:
STEP 7 — BLEU-4 / ROUGE-L
NLG metrics reported for cross-paper comparability only.
Note: BLEU / ROUGE measure n-gram overlap, not clinical correctness; they are secondary to F1-CheXbert and BERTScore in this project’s hierarchy.


Code
import evaluate

bleu_metric  = evaluate.load('bleu')
rouge_metric = evaluate.load('rouge')

# BLEU expects list-of-str predictions and list-of-list-of-str references
bleu_result = bleu_metric.compute(
    predictions=hypotheses,
    references=[[r] for r in references],
    max_order=params['eval']['bleu_max_order'],
)
bleu4 = bleu_result['bleu']

rouge_result = rouge_metric.compute(
    predictions=hypotheses,
    references=references,
    use_aggregator=True,
)
rouge_l = rouge_result['rougeL']

# Per-study BLEU-4 for distribution plot
bleu_per_study = []
for h, r in zip(hypotheses, references):
    try:
        b = bleu_metric.compute(predictions=[h], references=[[r]], max_order=4)['bleu']
    except Exception:
        b = 0.0
    bleu_per_study.append(b)
bleu_per_study = np.array(bleu_per_study)

print(f'BLEU-4  : {bleu4:.4f}')
print(f'ROUGE-L : {rouge_l:.4f}')

def _wrap(text: str, width: int = 80) -> str:
    import textwrap
    return '\n'.join(textwrap.wrap(text, width=width))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(14, 12))
tier_labels = ['Good (90th pct)', 'Median (50th pct)', 'Poor (10th pct)']

for ax, idx, tier in zip(axes, example_idx, tier_labels):

    bs      = bertscore_per_study[idx]
    bl      = bleu_per_study[idx]
    ref_txt = references[idx]
    hyp_txt = hypotheses[idx]

    table_data = [
        ['Reference', _wrap(ref_txt[:300], 95)],
        ['Generated', _wrap(hyp_txt[:300], 95)],
    ]
    table = ax.table(
        cellText=table_data,
        colWidths=[0.12, 0.88],
        cellLoc='left',
        loc='center',

    table[(1, 0)].set_facecolor('#d5f0d0')

title: 03 — QLoRA Fine-Tuning (Kaggle 2×T4)
text:
Purpose: Orchestrate the QLoRA training job.
All training logic lives in src/training/train.py; this notebook handles Kaggle-specific setup and produces report figures.
Two runs (both logged to W&B): | Run | Sampler | Goal | |—–|———|——| | qlora_uniform | --sampler uniform | No shift correction — fine-tuned baseline | | qlora_weighted | --sampler weighted | Importance-weighted — shift-corrected |
Instructions for Kaggle: 1. Upload this repo as a Kaggle Dataset or !git clone <repo> in the working dir. 2. Attach the IU CXR dataset: raddar/chest-xrays-indiana-university. 3. Enable GPU 2×T4, enable internet (for HuggingFace Hub + W&B). 4. Set WANDB_API_KEY in Kaggle Secrets, or run wandb login in cell 4. 5. Set HF_TOKEN in Kaggle Secrets (required for google/medgemma-4b-it). 6. Run all cells in order.
T4 optimisations applied automatically by train.py: - fp16 (T4 native tensor cores — bf16 is software-emulated on Turing) - torchrun --nproc_per_node=2 when 2 GPUs detected (DDP data parallelism, not pipeline) - max_seq_length=512 (shorter than default — IU reports are ~150-300 tokens) - batch_size=2 per GPU, grad_acc=8 → effective batch 32
Expected runtime: ~10–13 h total on 2×T4 (vs ~34 h without these optimisations).

import os
import sys
from pathlib import Path

ON_KAGGLE = Path('/kaggle').exists()

if ON_KAGGLE:
    # --- Kaggle layout ---

    REPO_ROOT = Path('/kaggle/working/ReportCXR')
    if not REPO_ROOT.exists():
        import subprocess
        subprocess.run(['git', 'clone', 'https://github.com/YOUR_USER/ReportCXR.git',
                        str(REPO_ROOT)], check=True)

    # Raw IU dataset is at this Kaggle input path:
    DATA_ROOT = Path('/kaggle/input/chest-xrays-indiana-university')

    os.chdir(REPO_ROOT)

from src.data.labels import CHEXBERT_LABELS
from src.training.sampler import build_sample_weights

train_df = pd.read_parquet(PROCESSED_DIR / 'train.parquet')
train_df = train_df[train_df['findings'].notna() & (train_df['findings'].str.strip() != '')]
label_matrix = train_df[CHEXBERT_LABELS].values.astype(float)

p_target   = params['sampler'].get('p_target', {})
weight_clip = float(params['sampler'].get('weight_clip', 10.0))

weights_uniform  = build_sample_weights(label_matrix, p_target=None, weight_clip=weight_clip)
weights_weighted = build_sample_weights(label_matrix, p_target=p_target, weight_clip=weight_clip)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 4))

for ax, weights, title, color in [
    (axes[0], weights_uniform,  'Uniform sampler weights', '#1f77b4'),
    (axes[1], weights_weighted, 'Importance weights (weighted sampler)', '#d62728'),
]:
    ax.hist(weights, bins=60, color=color, alpha=0.8, edgecolor='white', linewidth=0.3)
    ax.axvline(weights.mean(), color='black', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5,
               label=f'mean={weights.mean():.2f}')
    ax.axvline(np.median(weights), color='red', linestyle=':', linewidth=1.5,
               label=f'median={np.median(weights):.2f}')
    ax.set_xlabel('Sample weight')
    ax.set_ylabel('Training studies')
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.legend(fontsize=10)

# Annotate ESS for weighted sampler
ess = weights_weighted.sum()**2 / (weights_weighted**2).sum()
n   = len(weights_weighted)
axes[1].set_title(
    f'Importance weights — ESS={ess:.0f}/{n} ({100*ess/n:.1f}%)'
)
